metadata.title: [Re] Graph Edit Networks
organizations: University of Ljubljana, Faculty of Computer and Information Science

sections.title: Reproduced: Paaßen et. al. (ICLR, 2021)
content: The reproduced paper [bpa21] proposes a novel output layer for graph neural networks (GNNs), the graph edit network called (GEN). This layer yields a sequence of graph edits: node insertions, node deletions, node replacements, edge insertions and edge deletions. These finite sequences of edits, also referred to as edit scripts are general enough to describe any graph-to-graph transformation and are not only very interpretable for humans, but also computationally efficient.

The authors empirically underpin their theoretical claims about GENs by showing that they perform well in a series of graph time-series prediction tests. They define several data generating processes (DGPs), from which the GEN attempts to learn the user-defined mapping functions. Our work reproduces these experiments and evaluates the suitability of the DGPs for the conclusions made in the original paper.

figures.description: GEN model architecture showing original graph G transformed into predicted graph via node and edge scores and Alg. 1

sections.title: DGPs & Tests
content: The paper evaluated several key claims on synthetic datasets, generated with data generating rules, interpreted in the following figures.

figures.description: Graph cycles: A trivial set of user defined graph transformations without any logical rules.
Degree rules: A set of rules for node & edge insertions. Nodes with degree >= 3 are deleted.
Conway's Game of Life: A series based on node edits on a lattice graph.
Boolean Formulae: Random Boolean formulae simplified with logical rules.
Peano Addition: Series of tree graphs modelling addition problems via Peano's definition.

sections.title: [Re] Original experimental claims
content: [bpa21] makes three experimental conclusions based on a series of experiments. ✓ symbols below denote a successful reproduction of results.

✓ GENs outperform baselines (Variational Graph Autoencoders [KW16] & Variational Graph Recurrent nets [HHN19]) on time-series prediction tasks.✓ GENs achieve 100% accuracy on time-series prediction tasks on graphs, generated from Boolean Formulae & Peano Addition DGPs.? The runtime of forward passes of a GEN scales sub-quadratically as the number of nodes in a graph increases. The runtime of training back-passes scales approximately linearly.
While the focus here is speed, we have to note that the experiment in the original paper fails to optimize the criterion loss, thus casting doubt on the results.

tables.description: Log-log linear slope fit table for forward and backward pass directions.

figures.description: Runtime of forward and backward passes vs graph node count for flexible and constant edge filtering.
Training loss over iterations showing lack of optimization in original paper.

sections.title: Improving the experimental setup
content: The facts that graph-to-graph transitions are deterministic and that the space of unique graphs that may be generated by the DGPs is limited, arose some concern about the experimental setup.
We reproduced the claims (1) and (2) with the following adjustments:
Increased cardinality of test time series (5 -> 100)
Ensured no duplicates between train & test set
Used established random graph generation methods (ie. Erdős–Rényi)
We noticed that most sampled graphs in the tree dynamic graph experiments were un-simplifyable, so constraints were relaxed here.
Results were generally worse, but still within a reasonable margin of error.

tables.description: Table showing number of unique graphs for different DGPs like Graph cycles and Peano addition.

figures.description: Distribution of sampled TS lengths and probability of sampling unique simplifyable trees.

sections.title: Concluding reproduction notes
content: Our experimental results conclusively show that most of the claims in the original work hold. However, our work beyond the original paper emphasizes the need to pay attention to the experimental setup, especially when working with synthetically generated data, sampled from functions, rather than gathered from an external system.

sections.title: References
content: [bpa21] Paaßen, Benjamin, et al. "Graph edit networks." In: International Conference on Learning Representations. 2020
[Gar70] Martin Gardner. "The fantastic combinations of John Conway's new solitaire game of life." In: Sc. Am., 223:20-123, 1970
[KW16] Thomas N. Kipf and Max Welling. "Variational graph auto-encoders" In: Proceedings of the NIPS 2016 Workshop on Bayesian Deep Learning, 2016

[HHN19] Hajiramezanali, Ehsan, et. al. "Variational graph recurrent neural networks." In: Proceedings of the 32nd International Conference on Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2019), pp. 10700-10710, 2019

[LKF05] Leskovec, Jure, Jon Kleinberg, and Christos Faloutsos. "Graphs over time: densification laws, shrinking diameters and possible explanations." In: Proceedings of the eleventh ACM SIGKDD international conference on Knowledge discovery in data mining. 2005.
